---
title: "Multi-Class Classification"
engine: markdown
---

::: {.lm-hero}
[Chapter 4 · Classification]{.eyebrow}

# Multi-Class Classification

[Two routes from binary logistic regression to many classes: glue together a stack of independent yes/no classifiers, or fit one model whose outputs are forced to behave like a probability distribution.]{.dek}
:::

Most classification problems have more than two outcomes: ten digits, a handful of
document topics, a dozen species. Two strategies extend binary logistic regression to
$K$ classes. [One-vs-all]{.term} (OvA) trains $K$ separate binary classifiers, each
answering "this class or not," then predicts whichever fires most strongly.
[Softmax regression]{.term}, also called multinomial logistic regression, fits a single
model with $K$ score functions and squashes them into one probability vector.

The two often draw similar decision boundaries, so the interesting difference is not where
they split the plane but what their probabilities mean. OvA runs $K$ independent sigmoids
whose outputs need not sum to one; softmax couples the classes so the outputs always do.
That distinction is the point of this page.

::: {.defbox}
[Softmax]{.lbl}
[ P(y = k | x) = e<sup>z<sub>k</sub></sup> / &sum;<sub>j</sub> e<sup>z<sub>j</sub></sup>, &nbsp;&nbsp; z<sub>k</sub> = &theta;<sub>k</sub><sup>&#8868;</sup>x ]{.math}
:::

We work with a synthetic three-class problem in two dimensions so every boundary is visible.
Three Gaussian blobs sit at $(1,1)$, $(4,1)$, and $(2.5,4)$, with enough spread to overlap
at the edges. Both methods are built from scratch by gradient descent, the way the chapter
develops them; in production the binary fits are `glm(..., family = binomial)` and softmax
is `nnet::multinom`.

## The data

Fifty points per class, three classes, two features.

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## Python
```{pyodide}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)
n = 50
X = np.vstack([np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([1.0, 1.0]),
               np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([4.0, 1.0]),
               np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([2.5, 4.0])])
y = np.array([0] * n + [1] * n + [2] * n)
print(f"Dataset: {X.shape[0]} samples, {X.shape[1]} features, {len(np.unique(y))} classes")

colors = ["#076FA1", "#E3120B", "#9E4F00"]   # blue, red, orange (site identity)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5.5))
for c in range(3):
    m = y == c
    ax.scatter(X[m, 0], X[m, 1], c=colors[c], s=70, edgecolors="white",
               linewidths=1.2, label=f"Class {c}", zorder=3)
ax.set_xlabel("Feature x1"); ax.set_ylabel("Feature x2")
ax.legend(loc="upper right")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## R
```{webr}
set.seed(42)
n <- 50
mk <- function(cx, cy) cbind(rnorm(n, cx, 0.7), rnorm(n, cy, 0.7))
X <- rbind(mk(1, 1), mk(4, 1), mk(2.5, 4))
y <- rep(0:2, each = n)
cat(sprintf("Dataset: %d samples, %d features, %d classes\n",
            nrow(X), ncol(X), length(unique(y))))

cols <- c("#076FA1", "#E3120B", "#9E4F00")   # blue, red, orange (site identity)
plot(X[, 1], X[, 2], col = cols[y + 1], pch = 19, cex = 1.3,
     xlab = "Feature x1", ylab = "Feature x2")
legend("topright", legend = paste("Class", 0:2), col = cols, pch = 19)
```

:::

The two languages draw from different random number generators, so the point clouds, and the
accuracies that follow, differ in the last digit. The shapes and conclusions match.

## One-vs-all

Train one binary logistic classifier per class. For class $c$ the labels become
$1$ for "is class $c$" and $0$ for everything else; gradient descent on the log-loss finds
its weights. To classify a new point, evaluate all three sigmoids and take the
[argmax]{.term}. The shaded regions below are exactly that argmax over a grid.

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## Python
```{pyodide}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import ListedColormap

np.random.seed(42)
n = 50
X = np.vstack([np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([1.0, 1.0]),
               np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([4.0, 1.0]),
               np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([2.5, 4.0])])
y = np.array([0] * n + [1] * n + [2] * n)
K = 3

def sigmoid(z):
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-np.clip(z, -500, 500)))

def train_binary(Xd, yb, lr=1.0, iters=2000):
    theta = np.zeros(Xd.shape[1])
    for _ in range(iters):
        h = sigmoid(Xd @ theta)
        theta -= lr * (Xd.T @ (h - yb)) / len(yb)    # gradient of the log-loss
    return theta

Xd = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(y)), X])           # intercept column
all_theta = np.zeros((K, Xd.shape[1]))
for c in range(K):
    yb = (y == c).astype(float)                       # one class vs the rest
    all_theta[c] = train_binary(Xd, yb)
    acc = np.mean((sigmoid(Xd @ all_theta[c]) >= 0.5) == yb)
    print(f"Classifier {c} (Class {c} vs rest): accuracy = {acc:.1%}")

def predict_ova(Xnew):
    Xd = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(Xnew)), Xnew])
    P = sigmoid(Xd @ all_theta.T)                     # one sigmoid per class
    return np.argmax(P, axis=1), P

pred, _ = predict_ova(X)
print(f"Overall OvA accuracy: {np.mean(pred == y):.1%}")

x_min, x_max = X[:, 0].min() - 1, X[:, 0].max() + 1
y_min, y_max = X[:, 1].min() - 1, X[:, 1].max() + 1
xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(x_min, x_max, 200), np.linspace(y_min, y_max, 200))
Z, _ = predict_ova(np.column_stack([xx.ravel(), yy.ravel()]))
Z = Z.reshape(xx.shape)

colors = ["#076FA1", "#E3120B", "#9E4F00"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5.5))
ax.contourf(xx, yy, Z, alpha=0.25, cmap=ListedColormap(colors))
for c in range(K):
    m = y == c
    ax.scatter(X[m, 0], X[m, 1], c=colors[c], s=60, edgecolors="white",
               linewidths=1.2, zorder=3, label=f"Class {c}")
ax.set_xlabel("Feature x1"); ax.set_ylabel("Feature x2"); ax.legend(loc="upper right")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## R
```{webr}
set.seed(42)
n <- 50
mk <- function(cx, cy) cbind(rnorm(n, cx, 0.7), rnorm(n, cy, 0.7))
X <- rbind(mk(1, 1), mk(4, 1), mk(2.5, 4))
y <- rep(0:2, each = n)
K <- 3

sigmoid <- function(z) 1 / (1 + exp(-pmin(pmax(z, -500), 500)))

train_binary <- function(Xd, yb, lr = 1, iters = 2000) {
  theta <- rep(0, ncol(Xd))
  for (i in seq_len(iters)) {
    h <- sigmoid(Xd %*% theta)
    theta <- theta - lr * (t(Xd) %*% (h - yb)) / length(yb)   # log-loss gradient
  }
  as.vector(theta)
}

Xd <- cbind(1, X)                                    # intercept column
all_theta <- matrix(0, K, ncol(Xd))
for (c in 0:(K - 1)) {
  yb <- as.numeric(y == c)                           # one class vs the rest
  th <- train_binary(Xd, yb)
  all_theta[c + 1, ] <- th
  acc <- mean((sigmoid(Xd %*% th) >= 0.5) == yb)
  cat(sprintf("Classifier %d (Class %d vs rest): accuracy = %.1f%%\n", c, c, 100 * acc))
}

predict_ova <- function(Xnew) {
  P <- sigmoid(cbind(1, Xnew) %*% t(all_theta))       # one sigmoid per class
  list(pred = max.col(P) - 1, probs = P)
}
pred <- predict_ova(X)$pred
cat(sprintf("Overall OvA accuracy: %.1f%%\n", 100 * mean(pred == y)))

cols <- c("#076FA1", "#E3120B", "#9E4F00")
gx <- seq(min(X[, 1]) - 1, max(X[, 1]) + 1, length.out = 200)
gy <- seq(min(X[, 2]) - 1, max(X[, 2]) + 1, length.out = 200)
grid <- as.matrix(expand.grid(gx, gy))
Z <- matrix(predict_ova(grid)$pred, length(gx), length(gy))
image(gx, gy, Z, col = adjustcolor(cols, 0.3),
      xlab = "Feature x1", ylab = "Feature x2")
points(X[, 1], X[, 2], col = cols[y + 1], pch = 19, cex = 1.2)
legend("topright", legend = paste("Class", 0:2), col = cols, pch = 19)
```

:::

## Softmax regression

Softmax fits all classes at once. Each class gets a weight vector $\theta_k$, the scores
$z_k = \theta_k^\top x$ go through the softmax above, and gradient descent minimizes the
[cross-entropy loss]{.term}
$J(\Theta) = -\tfrac{1}{m}\sum_i \sum_k \mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)}=k\}\,\log P(y^{(i)}=k\mid x^{(i)})$.
The gradient has the same clean form as the binary case, $\tfrac{1}{m}X^\top(P - Y)$, with
$P$ the predicted probabilities and $Y$ the one-hot labels. Before training, a quick check
that softmax really does normalize: a row of raw scores comes out as probabilities that sum
to one.

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## Python
```{pyodide}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import ListedColormap

np.random.seed(42)
n = 50
X = np.vstack([np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([1.0, 1.0]),
               np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([4.0, 1.0]),
               np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([2.5, 4.0])])
y = np.array([0] * n + [1] * n + [2] * n)
K = 3

def softmax(Z):
    Z = Z - Z.max(axis=1, keepdims=True)             # subtract row max for stability
    E = np.exp(Z)
    return E / E.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)

demo = softmax(np.array([[2.0, 1.0, 0.1], [0.1, 0.1, 2.0]]))
print("softmax of two score rows:\n", demo.round(3))
print("row sums:", demo.sum(axis=1))

def train_softmax(X, y, lr=1.0, iters=1000):
    Xd = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(y)), X])
    Y = np.zeros((len(y), K)); Y[np.arange(len(y)), y] = 1   # one-hot labels
    Theta = np.zeros((Xd.shape[1], K))
    for _ in range(iters):
        P = softmax(Xd @ Theta)
        Theta -= lr * (Xd.T @ (P - Y)) / len(y)      # cross-entropy gradient
    return Theta

Theta = train_softmax(X, y)

def predict_sm(Xnew):
    Xd = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(Xnew)), Xnew])
    P = softmax(Xd @ Theta)
    return np.argmax(P, axis=1), P

pred, _ = predict_sm(X)
print(f"Softmax accuracy: {np.mean(pred == y):.1%}")

x_min, x_max = X[:, 0].min() - 1, X[:, 0].max() + 1
y_min, y_max = X[:, 1].min() - 1, X[:, 1].max() + 1
xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(x_min, x_max, 200), np.linspace(y_min, y_max, 200))
Z, _ = predict_sm(np.column_stack([xx.ravel(), yy.ravel()]))
Z = Z.reshape(xx.shape)

colors = ["#076FA1", "#E3120B", "#9E4F00"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5.5))
ax.contourf(xx, yy, Z, alpha=0.25, cmap=ListedColormap(colors))
for c in range(K):
    m = y == c
    ax.scatter(X[m, 0], X[m, 1], c=colors[c], s=60, edgecolors="white",
               linewidths=1.2, zorder=3, label=f"Class {c}")
ax.set_xlabel("Feature x1"); ax.set_ylabel("Feature x2"); ax.legend(loc="upper right")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## R
```{webr}
set.seed(42)
n <- 50
mk <- function(cx, cy) cbind(rnorm(n, cx, 0.7), rnorm(n, cy, 0.7))
X <- rbind(mk(1, 1), mk(4, 1), mk(2.5, 4))
y <- rep(0:2, each = n)
K <- 3

softmax <- function(Z) {
  Z <- sweep(Z, 1, apply(Z, 1, max), "-")            # subtract row max for stability
  E <- exp(Z)
  sweep(E, 1, rowSums(E), "/")
}

demo <- softmax(rbind(c(2, 1, 0.1), c(0.1, 0.1, 2)))
cat("softmax of two score rows:\n"); print(round(demo, 3))
cat("row sums:", rowSums(demo), "\n")

train_softmax <- function(X, y, lr = 1, iters = 1000) {
  Xd <- cbind(1, X)
  Y <- matrix(0, length(y), K); Y[cbind(seq_along(y), y + 1)] <- 1   # one-hot labels
  Theta <- matrix(0, ncol(Xd), K)
  for (i in seq_len(iters)) {
    P <- softmax(Xd %*% Theta)
    Theta <- Theta - lr * (t(Xd) %*% (P - Y)) / length(y)   # cross-entropy gradient
  }
  Theta
}
Theta <- train_softmax(X, y)

predict_sm <- function(Xnew) {
  P <- softmax(cbind(1, Xnew) %*% Theta)
  list(pred = max.col(P) - 1, probs = P)
}
cat(sprintf("Softmax accuracy: %.1f%%\n", 100 * mean(predict_sm(X)$pred == y)))

cols <- c("#076FA1", "#E3120B", "#9E4F00")
gx <- seq(min(X[, 1]) - 1, max(X[, 1]) + 1, length.out = 200)
gy <- seq(min(X[, 2]) - 1, max(X[, 2]) + 1, length.out = 200)
grid <- as.matrix(expand.grid(gx, gy))
Z <- matrix(predict_sm(grid)$pred, length(gx), length(gy))
image(gx, gy, Z, col = adjustcolor(cols, 0.3),
      xlab = "Feature x1", ylab = "Feature x2")
points(X[, 1], X[, 2], col = cols[y + 1], pch = 19, cex = 1.2)
legend("topright", legend = paste("Class", 0:2), col = cols, pch = 19)
```

:::

Both methods land near-perfect accuracy and carve up the plane almost identically. If
boundaries were the whole story, the choice would not matter.

## Where the methods part ways

The probabilities tell them apart. We evaluate both models at four points: the three class
centers and one point sitting between all three. At a center, the dominant classifier
saturates and the numbers nearly agree. At the in-between point, OvA reports three small,
unrelated confidences that sum to far less than one, because each sigmoid was trained in
ignorance of the others. Softmax, normalizing across classes, still returns a genuine
distribution. That guarantee is why softmax is the default whenever calibrated
probabilities matter, and why it reappears as the output layer of every classification
network in later chapters.

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## Python
```{pyodide}
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
n = 50; K = 3
X = np.vstack([np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([1.0, 1.0]),
               np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([4.0, 1.0]),
               np.random.randn(n, 2) * 0.7 + np.array([2.5, 4.0])])
y = np.array([0] * n + [1] * n + [2] * n)
Xd = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(y)), X])

def sigmoid(z): return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-np.clip(z, -500, 500)))
def softmax(Z):
    Z = Z - Z.max(axis=1, keepdims=True); E = np.exp(Z); return E / E.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)

# One-vs-all: K independent binary fits
all_theta = np.zeros((K, Xd.shape[1]))
for c in range(K):
    yb = (y == c).astype(float); th = np.zeros(Xd.shape[1])
    for _ in range(2000):
        th -= (Xd.T @ (sigmoid(Xd @ th) - yb)) / len(y)
    all_theta[c] = th

# Softmax: one coupled fit
Y = np.zeros((len(y), K)); Y[np.arange(len(y)), y] = 1
Theta = np.zeros((Xd.shape[1], K))
for _ in range(1000):
    P = softmax(Xd @ Theta); Theta -= (Xd.T @ (P - Y)) / len(y)

tests = np.array([[1.0, 1.0], [4.0, 1.0], [2.5, 4.0], [2.5, 2.0]])
Td = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(tests)), tests])
ova = sigmoid(Td @ all_theta.T)      # K independent sigmoids
sm = softmax(Td @ Theta)             # one normalized vector
print("point        OvA P(C0,C1,C2)        sum     Softmax P(C0,C1,C2)     sum")
for i, p in enumerate(tests):
    print(f"({p[0]:.1f},{p[1]:.1f})   "
          f"{ova[i,0]:.2f},{ova[i,1]:.2f},{ova[i,2]:.2f}   {ova[i].sum():.2f}    "
          f"{sm[i,0]:.2f},{sm[i,1]:.2f},{sm[i,2]:.2f}   {sm[i].sum():.2f}")
```

## R
```{webr}
set.seed(42)
n <- 50; K <- 3
mk <- function(cx, cy) cbind(rnorm(n, cx, 0.7), rnorm(n, cy, 0.7))
X <- rbind(mk(1, 1), mk(4, 1), mk(2.5, 4)); y <- rep(0:2, each = n)
Xd <- cbind(1, X)

sigmoid <- function(z) 1 / (1 + exp(-pmin(pmax(z, -500), 500)))
softmax <- function(Z) { Z <- sweep(Z, 1, apply(Z, 1, max), "-"); E <- exp(Z); sweep(E, 1, rowSums(E), "/") }

# One-vs-all: K independent binary fits
all_theta <- matrix(0, K, ncol(Xd))
for (c in 0:(K - 1)) {
  yb <- as.numeric(y == c); th <- rep(0, ncol(Xd))
  for (i in 1:2000) th <- th - (t(Xd) %*% (sigmoid(Xd %*% th) - yb)) / length(y)
  all_theta[c + 1, ] <- th
}

# Softmax: one coupled fit
Y <- matrix(0, length(y), K); Y[cbind(seq_along(y), y + 1)] <- 1
Theta <- matrix(0, ncol(Xd), K)
for (i in 1:1000) { P <- softmax(Xd %*% Theta); Theta <- Theta - (t(Xd) %*% (P - Y)) / length(y) }

tests <- rbind(c(1, 1), c(4, 1), c(2.5, 4), c(2.5, 2))
Td <- cbind(1, tests)
ova <- sigmoid(Td %*% t(all_theta))    # K independent sigmoids
sm <- softmax(Td %*% Theta)            # one normalized vector
cat("point        OvA P(C0,C1,C2)        sum     Softmax P(C0,C1,C2)     sum\n")
for (i in 1:nrow(tests)) {
  cat(sprintf("(%.1f,%.1f)   %.2f,%.2f,%.2f   %.2f    %.2f,%.2f,%.2f   %.2f\n",
              tests[i, 1], tests[i, 2], ova[i, 1], ova[i, 2], ova[i, 3], sum(ova[i, ]),
              sm[i, 1], sm[i, 2], sm[i, 3], sum(sm[i, ])))
}
```

:::

Read the last row of the table: the in-between point. The OvA "probabilities" sum to a
fraction, a number with no probabilistic meaning, while the softmax row sums to one. The two
languages start from different random data, so the digits differ, but both show the same gap.
One-vs-all is simple and trivially parallel; softmax costs a coupled fit and returns
probabilities you can trust.

::: {.explore}
[Try it]{.lbl}
In the softmax cells, divide the scores by a [temperature]{.term} $T$ before the softmax
(replace `Xd @ Theta` with `(Xd @ Theta) / T`) and watch the predicted probabilities. Small
$T$ sharpens them toward a one-hot vote; large $T$ flattens them toward uniform. This is the
same temperature knob that controls how adventurous a language model's next-token sampling is
in Chapter 12.
:::
